findings.title: Actor and surface contract is not explicit enough to design against
findings.detail: The proposal introduces actor taxonomy, surface taxonomy, and approval/clarify/review semantics, but it does not state the governing rules the design must preserve: whether permissions are determined by actor only, whether surfaces can constrain or extend behavior, and which actor/surface combinations are valid. Without those invariants, actor-surface-model, workflow-run-state, and review-orchestration can each implement different semantics. Add an explicit rules section or matrix with representative scenarios and valid pairings.

findings.title: Existing surface-aware spec is not reconciled with the new model
findings.detail: The Why section identifies agent-context-template as the current place where surface separation already exists, but the impact list does not say whether that spec will be aligned to the new surface taxonomy or intentionally left unchanged. This creates a likely source of spec drift between old and new surface terminology. Either include the affected spec in scope or state a clear non-goal and compatibility expectation.

findings.title: Provenance requirements are missing compatibility boundaries
findings.detail: The proposal says each transition should record an actor, but it does not define how legacy runs, system-generated transitions, or unknown actors are handled, and it is unclear whether surface provenance is intentionally excluded from persisted state. Those constraints materially affect schema and migration design. Add explicit backward-compatibility/defaulting rules and clarify what provenance must, may, and must not be persisted.

findings.title: Gated decision semantics are still incomplete
findings.detail: The proposal now says permissions are actor-driven and allows `ai-agent` approval only when delegated, but it still does not define the full contract for gated decisions: `reject` is mentioned in the rules text but omitted from the permission matrix, and `delegated` approval has no minimum invariant for who can grant it, what scope it has, or whether the delegating authority must be auditable. Because approval/rejection are core workflow gates, design is still missing a complete governing rule set for review-orchestration and provenance. Add explicit reject semantics and the minimum delegation/accountability requirements.

findings.title: AI review `approve` conflicts with delegated approval rule
findings.detail: `approve (delegated)` says an ai-agent may execute `approve` only when explicitly delegated by a human, but `review decision` then gives ai-agent reviewers the same `approve / request_changes / block` decision vocabulary as human reviewers without stating whether AI `approve` is advisory-only or a binding gate. Design cannot tell whether an undelegated AI review `approve` should advance the workflow, be treated as a recommendation, or be rejected. Separate review outcome labels from gated approvals or explicitly bind review `approve` to the same delegation rule.

REQUEST_CHANGES||R2-F04|proposal.md|completeness|medium|Gated decision semantics are still incomplete||R3-F05|proposal.md|consistency|medium|AI review `approve` conflicts with delegated approval rule
